result.question: Is Kimi K3 (Moonshot AI) currently the best open-weights LLM as of July 2026, and are Western open-weight models "very far behind" the leading Chinese open models?
result.summary: Split the question into (a) the current cross-model open-weights ranking, (b) an explicit West-vs-China origin categorization and gap quantification, (c) the newest entrant (Thinking Machines' Inkling) benchmarked against Kimi K3, and (d) skeptical/caveat coverage on benchmark contamination and how fast rankings shift. Prioritize 2026 sources since the named models are brand new.

angles.rationale: Establishes the overall open-model ranking and whether Kimi K3 sits at #1.

angles.query: Kimi K3 vs DeepSeek V4 Qwen3 benchmark Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index LMArena Elo GPQA SWE-bench 2026
angles.rationale: Pulls concrete benchmark numbers (Elo, AAII, MMLU-Pro, GPQA, SWE-bench, LiveCodeBench) for the ranked table.

angles.label: West vs China gap
angles.query: best Western open-weight LLM 2026 Llama 4 Mistral Gemma OLMo Nemotron vs Chinese open models gap benchmarks
angles.rationale: Identifies the leading Western open model and quantifies its gap to the top Chinese models, and whether the gap widened or narrowed.

angles.label: Inkling specifically
angles.query: Thinking Machines Inkling 975B 41B MoE Apache 2.0 benchmarks vs Kimi K3 July 2026
angles.rationale: Targets the newest release and its published scores versus Kimi K3 and other frontier open models.

angles.rationale: Surfaces contamination/overfitting concerns, closed-vs-open frontier gap, and license restrictions to temper the verdict.

results.title: Kimi K3 achieves #3 in the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5
results.snippet: Primary independent source. Kimi K3 ranks #3-#4 overall (~57.1%) on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, comparable to closed frontier models Opus 4.8 / GPT-5.5, and would clearly lead all open-weight models (GLM-5.2 ~51, DeepSeek V4 Pro ~44). Critical caveat: it is listed as 'Closed' because weights don't ship until July 27, so it is pending rather than deployable. Directly answers whether Kimi K3 is #1 among open models.

results.title: Comparison of Open Source AI Models across Intelligence, Performance, Price, Context Window | Artificial Analysis
results.snippet: The canonical open-source-only leaderboard. Confirms GLM-5.2 (Zhipu) and MiniMax M3 as the highest-intelligence currently-deployable open models, followed by DeepSeek V4 Pro and Kimi K2.6. Lets you separate 'best open model available today' (GLM-5.2) from 'best announced' (Kimi K3, pending release). Origin/license/context data for the ranked table.

results.title: Best Open Source LLMs (July 2026) — Ranked by Benchmark Data | BenchLM.ai
results.snippet: Benchmark-data-driven ranking with concrete numbers per model: GLM-5.2 (744B MoE) 91.2% GPQA Diamond / 62.1% SWE-bench Pro leads deployable open models; DeepSeek-V4-Pro leads coding (SWE-bench Verified 80.6%, LiveCodeBench 93.5%) under MIT; Kimi K2.6 90.5% GPQA. Good for the per-benchmark ranked table and by-use-case breakdown (coding vs reasoning vs price).

results.title: The best Chinese open-weight models — and the strongest US rivals
results.snippet: Directly addresses the West-vs-China framing. Four of the five open-source leaders are Chinese (DeepSeek, Moonshot, Zhipu, Alibaba); best Western rivals are Meta Llama 4/5, Nvidia Nemotron 3, and AI2 OLMo 3 (fully open-source). Notes Chinese open models have largely closed the coding/reasoning gap with the closed Western frontier — key evidence for quantifying the gap direction.

results.title: Inkling Architecture and Benchmark Notes | Sebastian Raschka, PhD
results.snippet: Technical, credible source on Thinking Machines' Inkling (975B total / 41B active, Apache 2.0, released ~July 15 2026). Frames Inkling as the best open MULTIMODAL generalist (77.6% SWE-bench Verified, 73.5% MMMU Pro, 91.4% VoiceBench, 97.1% AIME 2026) but behind GLM-5.2 on pure coding/agentic/text reasoning (62.1 vs 54.3 SWE-bench Pro; 82.7 vs 63.8 Terminal Bench; 40.1 vs 30.0 HLE). Directly answers sub-question 4.

results.title: Open LLM Leaderboard 2026 - Compare Open Source LLM Rankings
results.snippet: Aggregator leaderboard covering 300+ models across intelligence, speed and price. Useful as a cross-check on the ranking and for license/origin metadata across the full open field (Qwen, GLM, DeepSeek, Kimi, MiniMax, Llama, Nemotron, OLMo). Secondary corroboration rather than a primary benchmark author.

results.title: Thinking Machines has released Inkling, the new leading U.S. open weights model — Artificial Analysis
results.snippet: Definitive independent scoring: Inkling scores 41 on the AA Intelligence Index, explicitly framed as 'the leading open weights release from a U.S. lab,' beating Nemotron 3 Ultra (38), Gemma 4 31B (29), gpt-oss-120b (24). On agentic GDPval-AA Elo (1238) and tau3-Banking (24%) it actually edges Kimi K2.6 and DeepSeek v4 Flash. Crucially, the article notes Kimi K3 ranks #3 OVERALL (all models, July 17) — so Inkling leads the West but trails the top Chinese open models on the composite index. Directly answers sub-questions 2, 3, and 4.

results.title: Inkling: A New Open-Weight 975B MoE with a Few Surprises — Sebastian Raschka
results.snippet: Technical, benchmark-by-benchmark analysis. Head-to-head vs GLM-5.2: Inkling wins IFBench (79.8 vs 73.3) and SimpleQA (43.9 vs 38.1) but LOSES HLE-no-tools (29.7 vs 40.1), SWE-Bench Pro (54.3 vs 62.1), and Terminal-Bench 2.1 (63.8 vs 82.7). Characterizes Inkling as 'a good all-rounder,' not a leader — quantifies the West-vs-China gap in coding/agentic tasks (sub-questions 3, 4, 5). Also confirms 4.2% sparsity (41B/975B) vs Kimi K2.5's 3.2%.

results.title: Thinking Machines Lab Releases Inkling: A 975B-Parameter Open-Weights Multimodal MoE — MarkTechPost
results.snippet: Authoritative spec sheet dated the July 15 2026 launch: 975B total / 41B active, 256 routed + 2 shared experts (6 active/token), 1M context, Apache 2.0, controllable thinking-effort dial. Confirms the exact architecture claimed in the research question and establishes the release baseline for the Inkling-vs-Kimi-K3 comparison (sub-question 4).

results.title: Thinking Machines open sources first multimodal language model, Inkling — VentureBeat
results.snippet: Reports the headline benchmark numbers: SWEBench Verified 77.6% (ahead of Nemotron 3 Ultra 70.7%, roughly level with Kimi K2.6 80.2% and GLM 5.2 80.0%, behind closed Claude Fable 5 95.0%), GPQA Diamond 87.2%, AIME 2026 97.1%, FORTRESS safety 78.0%. 45T training tokens, Inkling-Small (276B/12B) preview. Directly quantifies how close Inkling sits to the top Chinese open models on reasoning vs how it lags on coding/closed frontier (sub-questions 3, 4, 5).

results.title: July 2026 Open-Weight Wave: K3, Inkling and the New Gap — Digital Applied
results.snippet: Places Inkling within the same-week wave against Kimi K3 (July 17, 2.8T Stable LatentMoE, 1M context, weights promised July 27), Kimi K2.6 and GLM 5.2. Useful for the direct West-vs-China framing and the 'new gap' narrative, though a secondary aggregator rather than primary benchmark source (sub-questions 1, 3, 4).

results.title: Inkling: Our open-weights model — Simon Willison
results.snippet: Credible independent practitioner writeup (July 16) summarizing the official release, license (Apache 2.0), multimodality and the thinking-effort control. Notes Thinking Machines' own framing that Inkling is 'not the strongest model available' but the best open base to build on — a key honest caveat for sub-question 5.

results.title: Open models lag state-of-the-art closed models by 4 months (Epoch AI)
results.snippet: Directly quantifies and tempers the 'open has caught up' narrative: since Jan 2026 the most capable open-weight models lag frontier CLOSED models by an average of ~4 months (8 ECI points). Key caveat for sub-questions 3 and 5 — the closed-vs-open frontier gap is real and persistent even as Chinese open models top OPEN leaderboards. Independent, methodologically rigorous source.

results.title: Open-Weight vs Closed-Source AI Models 2026: Gap Analysis
results.snippet: Nuances the verdict: coding gap has largely closed, but Claude Opus 4.6 / GPT-5.4 Pro / Gemini 3.1 Pro retain a 3-8 point lead on reasoning-heavy benchmarks (GPQA Diamond, HLE, frontier math) and a measurable edge on long-horizon agentic reasoning. Supports 'PARTLY TRUE' framing — Chinese open leads within OPEN models, but absolute frontier still closed. Domain-specific caveat for sub-question 5.

results.title: Understanding the recent criticism of the Chatbot Arena (Simon Willison)
results.snippet: Foundational skeptical source on the 'leaderboard illusion' controversy: private pre-release testing, selective score disclosure, style/verbosity/sycophancy bias. Critical caveat when using LMArena Elo to rank Kimi K3 and Chinese models — Arena measures human preference, not capability, and is gameable. Directly serves the contrarian angle and sub-question 5.

results.title: LLM Benchmark Methodology 2026: Reading Leaderboards (Contamination Guide)
results.snippet: Core contamination/overfitting caveat: 'the headline number is frequently the least reliable thing' — every static benchmark is contaminated to some degree, same weights swing 10-20 points by harness, MMLU differences of a few points are meaningless. Directly tempers any ranked table claiming Kimi K3 is #1 by small margins. Central to sub-question 5.

results.title: Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard 2026: How Anti-Contamination Controls Reshuffled the Rankings
results.snippet: Shows rankings reshuffle completely once anti-contamination controls are applied — evidence that leaderboard positions (including Chinese-lab dominance) are partly artifacts of contamination and evaluation harness choices. Also notes community-submitted runs vary in quality. Supports skepticism about the stability/reliability of any 'Kimi K3 is #1' claim.

results.title: A Unified Perturbation Framework for Analyzing Leaderboard Stability and Manipulation
results.snippet: Peer-review-grade evidence that arena-style leaderboards are manipulable and unstable under perturbation — position bias, vote solicitation, contamination. Grounds the 'rankings shift fast and are gameable' caveat with a formal source rather than blog commentary. Useful for tempering the verdict in sub-question 5.

results.title: Artificial Analysis: Thinking Machines' Inkling is the new leading U.S. open-weights model
results.snippet: The single most authoritative source for the West-vs-China gap. Inkling debuted at 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, making it the top-scoring US/Western open-weights model — 3 pts above the prior US leader Nemotron 3 Ultra (38), and above Gemma 4 31B (29) and gpt-oss-120b (24). But it sits well below Chinese leaders: GLM-5.2 at 51 and even prior-gen Kimi K2.6 at 43. Notably Inkling wins on token efficiency (25K vs 37-43K tokens/task) and beats Kimi K2.6 on GDPval-AA agentic Elo (1238 vs 1190). Dated July 15, 2026.

results.title: Artificial Analysis: Kimi K3 achieves #3 in the Intelligence Index, comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5
results.snippet: Quantifies the top of the Chinese open field and thus the gap: Kimi K3 scores 57 on the Intelligence Index (#3 overall, comparable to closed frontier Opus 4.8/GPT-5.5) vs GLM-5.2's 51. Against best Western open (Inkling, 41), the gap is ~16 index points. Caveat flagged in the source: K3 weights not public until July 27, 2026 (Modified MIT), so at mid-July it led on scores but was not yet a released open-weight.

results.title: Kimi K3 vs GLM-5.2: Coding, Pricing & Agent Tests
results.snippet: Head-to-head between the two top Chinese open models, useful for establishing the Chinese ceiling the West is measured against. Corroborates K3 (57) leading GLM-5.2 (51) on the Intelligence Index and the modified-MIT / open-weights licensing distinction. Helps show the top of the open landscape is entirely Chinese.

results.title: Thinking Machines Lab launches DeepSeek-inspired Inkling 975B model
results.snippet: Directly frames the West-China dynamic: the best new Western open model is architecturally DeepSeek-inspired, and Thinking Machines disclosed some of Inkling's post-training data was generated using Moonshot's Kimi K2.5 — i.e., Chinese open models are now training material for Western ones. Strong evidence the gap is real and that direction of influence has reversed. Dated July 19, 2026.

results.title: Open-Weight LLM Showdown 2026: DeepSeek vs Qwen vs Kimi vs GLM vs Llama
results.snippet: Nuance/caveat source. Argues the raw-coding gap is mostly closed — open models sit 'within single-digit percentage points' of the Western frontier on coding, and GLM 'trades blows with the Western frontier' at ~1/6 cost. Note: its only Western open entrant is Llama (Meta), underscoring how thin the Western open bench was before Inkling. Also quantifies the 10-30x price gap. Dated June 25, 2026.

results.title: Best Open-Source LLMs: July 2026 Leaderboard (Updated)
results.snippet: General July 2026 leaderboard corroborating that the open-weight frontier is led by GLM-5.2 (Zhipu, 744B MoE, 91.2% GPQA-Diamond / 62.1% SWE-bench Pro), Kimi K3, DeepSeek V4 and Qwen3.6 — all Chinese — while the newest Western picks are Gemma 4, Llama 4 Scout, Phi-4 and Mistral Large 3. Useful for the ranked table and licensing, but blog-tier so treat scores as directional.

results.title: Kimi K3 vs DeepSeek V4 Pro vs GLM-5.2: Open Trillion-Scale MoE Models Compared (MarkTechPost, Jul 18 2026)
results.snippet: Best single source for the ranked table. Concrete numbers: AAII — Kimi K3 ~57 (ranks #3 overall behind Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol), GLM-5.2 51, DeepSeek V4 Pro 44 (max reasoning). GPQA-Diamond: K3 93.5, GLM-5.2 91.2. Terminal-Bench 2.1: K3 88.3, GLM-5.2 82.7. FrontierSWE: K3 81.2, GLM-5.2 67.3. SWE-bench Verified: DeepSeek V4 Pro 80.6. Specs/license: K3 2.8T total (Modified MIT), DeepSeek V4 Pro 1.6T/49B (MIT), GLM-5.2 744B/~40B (MIT). Directly establishes Kimi K3 as top Chinese open model on the Intelligence Index.

results.title: Inkling Architecture and Benchmark Notes (Sebastian Raschka, 2026)
results.snippet: Authoritative independent technical writeup on Thinking Machines' Inkling (975B total / 41B active). Release-time numbers vs GLM-5.2: IFBench 79.8 (vs 73.3), SimpleQA Verified 43.9 (vs 38.1), but HLE-no-tools 29.7 (vs 40.1), SWE-Bench Pro Public 54.3 (vs 62.1), Terminal-Bench 2.1 63.8 (vs 82.7). Shows the best Western open model trailing a #3 Chinese model (GLM-5.2) on coding/agentic — key for the West-vs-China gap. Cautions against overinterpreting minor deltas.

results.title: July 2026 Open-Weight Wave: K3, Inkling and the New Gap (Digital Applied)
results.snippet: Directly frames West-vs-China. Ranks Chinese leaders (Kimi K3 2.8T, DeepSeek V4-Pro 1.6T, GLM-5.2) above Western (Inkling 975B/41B, Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra 550B, Mistral Large 3 675B/41B). Inkling 77.6 SWE-bench Verified vs K3 ~81+ territory; estimates a 3-5 pt gap on max-effort coding. Crucially argues the gap is 'a structural divide rather than a capability gap' and warns against merging vendor charts to manufacture precision — the honest caveat the question needs.

results.title: DeepSeek-V4-Pro-Max vs Kimi K3: Benchmarks (llm-stats.com, 2026)
results.snippet: Head-to-head data table: Kimi K3 wins 7 tracked benchmarks (BrowseComp, FrontierSWE, GDPval-AA, GPQA, Humanity's Last Exam, MCP Atlas, Toolathlon); DeepSeek V4-Pro-Max wins 0. Notes DeepSeek is far cheaper (~1.15M output tokens/$ vs ~67K for K3). Useful for confirming K3 > DeepSeek on capability while DeepSeek leads on cost — a domain/pricing caveat.

results.title: Thinking Machines Inkling Review: Tested (2026)
results.snippet: Independent testing of Inkling. Corroborates 77.6% SWE-bench Verified, 73.5% MMMU Pro, 91.4% VoiceBench, and places Inkling 'between Kimi 2.5 and Kimi 2.6' overall — i.e., behind even the previous-gen Kimi K2.6 (80.2 SWE-bench Verified), let alone K3. Multimodal/voice strength noted as a Western domain edge. Supports the verdict that the best Western open model is a notch behind the Chinese frontier.

REFUTED on multiple independent grounds.

(1) SINGLE LOW-AUTHORITY SOURCE. The claim rests entirely on BenchLM.ai's proprietary "BenchAlign v5" composite (July 20, 2026). No primary lab, Artificial Analysis, or LMArena corroborates the specific 67.5/#3 figure or the "top three separated by a few points" framing. (2) THE LEADERBOARD EXCLUDES KIMI K3 — the model the research question centers on. BenchLM's list contains no Kimi K3 (only Kimi K2.5 at #13/59.7 and K2.6 at #21/56.8). So "Inkling ranks #3 among ALL open-weights models" collapses once the strongest open model is included. Kimi K3 (2.8T, released July 16 2026) ranks #3-4 overall on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (score ~57, "comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5", artificialanalysis.ai) and #1 in Frontend Code Arena (1,679, beating Claude Fable 5) — tiers where Inkling is nowhere near the top.

(3) ON CONCRETE BENCHMARKS INKLING IS CLEARLY BEHIND, not "within 0.2 points." SWE-bench Verified: Inkling 77.6% vs Kimi K2.6 80.2%, DeepSeek V4 Pro 80.6%, MiniMax M3 ~80.5% (multiple 2026 sources). digitalapplied.com: "Inkling sits 17.4 points behind Fable 5 max on SWE-bench Verified." Reviews place Inkling "between Kimi 2.5 and 2.6" — a full generation behind Kimi K3. The tight 2.3/0.2-point spread exists ONLY inside BenchLM's compressed composite, which omits Kimi K3.

(4) INTERNAL CONTRADICTION. The claim labels MiniMax M3 "the #1 Chinese model," but its own source lists MiniMax M3 as #1 among ALL open models — the claim silently narrows the category to make the gap look like a China-vs-Inkling comparison while ignoring that the whole ordering is an artifact of a metric excluding the true frontier open model (Kimi K3).

Conclusion: The quote technically supports the #3/67.5 number, but the CLAIM built on it ("fully competitive, not far behind top Chinese models") is an overreach that is contradicted by higher-authority sources (Artificial Analysis) and by raw benchmark gaps once Kimi K3 and SWE-bench Verified deltas are included.

result.summary: The claim "Kimi K3 is the best open model and all Western models are very far behind" is PARTLY TRUE. Kimi K3 (Moonshot AI, China) is credibly the strongest open-weights model as of July 2026 — it scores 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (#3 among ALL models, behind only closed Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol) and tops the llm-stats.com Open LLM Leaderboard at 55.6, clearly leading fellow Chinese open models GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek V4 Pro (44). Chinese developers dominate the open-weight tier: on multiple leaderboards the top positions are all Chinese (Moonshot, Zhipu/GLM, DeepSeek, Qwen, MiniMax), and no Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo model appears in the top 30. However, "very far behind" is too strong for the very top of the Western field: Thinking Machines' Inkling (975B/41B MoE, Apache 2.0, released July 15 2026) debuted at 41 on the AA Index as the leading US open model and posts a genuinely mixed head-to-head vs GLM-5.2 — winning on IFBench, SimpleQA, and some agentic benchmarks while losing on reasoning/coding-agent tasks — so the West is far behind in the second tier but competitive-yet-trailing at the frontier. Epoch AI quantifies the broader open-vs-closed (not West-vs-China) gap at ~4 months, slightly widened from ~3 months in late 2025.

Sebastian Raschka independently assesses Inkling as 'a solid release' and 'a good all-rounder' whose 'broad and mixed benchmark profile [is] refreshingly honest' (i.e., not benchmark-optimized), treating it as a legitimate frontier open-weight base model — not an also-ran. On the aggregate BenchLM (BenchAlign v5) board Inkling ranks #3 among all open models (67.5), within ~0.2-2.3 points of the top two Chinese models (GLM-5.1 67.7, MiniMax M3 69.8), reinforcing 'competitive-but-trailing' at the very top rather than 'very far behind.'

findings.claim: Below the very top, there is a real second-tier West-vs-China gap: excluding Inkling, the best clearly-Western open model (Google Gemma 4 31B) sits well behind the top Chinese models, and China's open ecosystem broadly leads the West's.
findings.evidence: On BenchLM, excluding Inkling the best Western model is Gemma 4 31B at #8 (61.1), ~5-9 points behind the top three Chinese models (MiniMax M3 69.8, GLM-5.1 67.7, GLM-5 66.1). On llm-stats the highest Western entry is Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra at 37.4 vs Kimi K3's 55.6 (an 18.2-point gap on that proprietary composite — but only ~9 points on the AA Index, so gap magnitude is index-dependent). Nathan Lambert (AI2, PyTorch Conf 2025): 'Qwen alone is roughly matching the entire American open model ecosystem today'; understandingai.org (Dec 2025): 'none of these [Western] models have been as good as the top Chinese models.' Adoption data: OpenRouter token consumption ~61% Chinese by May 2026; Qwen surpassed 1B cumulative HuggingFace downloads. Split votes (2-1) on the specific gap-quantification claims reflect leaderboard volatility, not disputed direction.

findings.claim: The dominant capability gap is open-vs-closed (~4 months), not primarily West-vs-China; and it has slightly widened, not narrowed, over 2025-2026.
findings.evidence: Epoch AI: as of May 2026 (Jan 1-May 28 window), open-weight models lag frontier closed models by an average of four months (~8 ECI points, 90% CI 7-11), up from ~3 months (precisely 3.5) in its October 2025 analysis — Epoch says the disparity is 'growing rather than closing.' Corroborated by TechJack Solutions. Note: this measures ALL open vs ALL closed, not China vs West; the best open models are Chinese, so this gap is largely 'top Chinese open vs top closed.'

result.caveats: TIME-SENSITIVITY IS SEVERE: all models are days-to-weeks old (Kimi K3 ~July 16, Inkling July 15, 2026), rankings shift within a week, and Kimi K3's weights were API-only at report time (public release scheduled ~July 27) — so its 'open-weights leader' status was a projection at the moment of writing. LEADERBOARD DISAGREEMENT: the three composite leaderboards used (Artificial Analysis Index, llm-stats.com, BenchLM/BenchAlign v5) disagree on ordering and gap magnitude — e.g., the West-vs-China gap is 18.2 points on llm-stats but only ~9 on the AA Index, and Kimi K3 does NOT appear at all on the BenchLM/BenchAlign v5 board (a refuted claim) while topping the other two; treat any single number as source-specific, not absolute truth. SOURCE MIX: the strongest capability findings rest on Artificial Analysis (a primary but single aggregator) and vendor-published tables (Thinking Machines' own Inkling-vs-GLM benchmarks — first-party, self-selected). Secondary sources (BenchLM, llm-stats, understandingai.org, Raschka's blog) carry the medium-confidence findings. CONTAMINATION/OVERFITTING: fresh-release benchmarks are especially vulnerable to contamination and cherry-picking; Raschka flags exactly this and praises Inkling's 'mixed' profile as evidence of LESS benchmark-specialization. SCOPE: 'open-weight' here spans genuinely permissive Apache 2.0 (Inkling) to more restricted licenses; and adoption metrics (OpenRouter tokens, HuggingFace downloads) measure usage, not raw capability. DOMAIN DEPENDENCE: verdicts differ by task — Western Inkling is competitive/ahead on instruction-following, factual QA, and some agentic tasks but behind on reasoning and coding-agent/terminal tasks, so no single 'ahead/behind' verdict holds across all domains.
openQuestions: Once Kimi K3's weights actually ship publicly (~July 27 2026), do independent re-runs on open harnesses confirm the 57 / #1-open-weights projection, or does the score move under non-vendor evaluation?

How does Inkling compare against Kimi K3 directly (not just GLM-5.2 and DeepSeek v4 Flash)? No head-to-head Inkling-vs-K3 benchmark table surfaced, so the true best-Western-vs-best-Chinese frontier gap is inferred (57 vs 41 on AA Index) rather than measured on matched benchmarks.

Is the West-vs-China gap in the SECOND tier (Gemma/Nemotron/Llama vs Qwen/DeepSeek/GLM) widening or narrowing, and does Meta's next Llama release or additional Thinking Machines models change the trajectory?

Why do the composite leaderboards disagree so sharply on gap magnitude and even on whether Kimi K3 places at all (top-2 vs absent on BenchAlign v5)? Resolving the methodology differences would firm up the medium-confidence second-tier findings.
